metadata.description: Held-out negotiation strategic_concession_making pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the strategic_concession_making vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Strategic Concession-Making
dimensions.description: Making concessions conditionally, reciprocally, and with deliberate pacing; never giving something for nothing

pairs.context: A buyer has asked for a 10% discount and you want to close the deal but not give away margin.
pairs.positive: I can offer 7% if you're willing to pay within 14 days instead of 30. That shortens my cash cycle and makes the concession workable on my side.
pairs.negative: Okay, 10% is fine. I want to get this closed and it's not worth losing the deal over the margin.

pairs.context: A client is asking you to add an additional deliverable to the project scope without changing the fee.
pairs.positive: I can add that deliverable, but not without adjusting somewhere else. Either we increase the fee by the amount it costs, or we agree to remove something from the current scope. Which would you prefer?
pairs.negative: Sure, I'll fit it in. I want you to be satisfied with what we deliver and I'd rather add it than have you feel like you didn't get everything you needed.

pairs.context: During a lease negotiation, the landlord has offered one month of free rent; you want three months.
pairs.positive: One month is a start, but I need three to make the numbers work on my side. I'm prepared to accept one month now and sign a longer lease term if you can get to three — that gives you the security of a longer commitment.
pairs.negative: One month is fine. I was hoping for more but I don't want to risk losing the space over it.

pairs.context: A distributor is asking for improved margin; you need something in return.
pairs.positive: I can improve the margin by two points if you commit to a minimum annual volume. I need to know the revenue is there before I change the economics.
pairs.negative: I can improve the margin. I want this partnership to work and the two points won't kill us.

pairs.context: A supplier is asking for a price increase on a long-running contract.
pairs.positive: I'm open to discussing a price adjustment. Before I agree to any number, I need a multi-year contract extension and a price stability clause for the following period. I'll give on the current rate if you give me predictability going forward.
pairs.negative: I suppose prices do go up. I'll agree to the increase to maintain a good relationship. The alternative is a disruptive supplier change.

pairs.context: A job candidate is pushing for a higher starting salary than budgeted.
pairs.positive: I can't move on the base salary, but I can offer a guaranteed six-month review with a clear target that, if hit, gets you to the number you want. I need you to start at the budgeted figure, but I'll put the path to your target in writing.
pairs.negative: You drive a hard bargain. I'll try to find the extra from somewhere — I'd rather have you than lose you over the gap.

pairs.context: A buyer is asking for a faster delivery schedule and you need something in exchange.
pairs.positive: I can accelerate the schedule by two weeks if you can give me a confirmed purchase order today rather than the letter of intent. I need the commitment to justify pulling the team off other work.
pairs.negative: I'll do what I can to speed it up. I know it's important to you and I'll push the team to make it happen.

pairs.context: A partner firm is requesting that you cover all costs of a joint marketing campaign.
pairs.positive: I'll cover the production costs if you take the media spend. I'm not willing to front the entire campaign, but I'm happy to make this work if we split it along those lines.
pairs.negative: If it helps move the partnership forward, I can probably absorb the campaign costs this time. We can talk about splitting it more fairly next round.

pairs.context: During an M&A negotiation the acquirer wants more representations and warranties than you think are reasonable.
pairs.positive: I can give you stronger reps on the areas where you've identified specific risk — financials and IP. In return, I need the indemnity cap raised to make the overall exposure manageable on my side.
pairs.negative: I suppose I can give them what they're asking for on the reps. I want this deal to close and pushing back on this might create unnecessary friction.

pairs.context: A client wants you to agree to unlimited revisions for a fixed-price design project.
pairs.positive: I can give you two additional revision rounds beyond my standard three, making it five total. Beyond that, further revisions would be at an agreed hourly rate. That's my offer — five rounds included.
pairs.negative: Fine, unlimited revisions. I want you to love the final product and if that's what it takes, I'll agree to it.

pairs.context: A wholesaler is asking for a lower unit price in exchange for a larger order.
pairs.positive: I can get to the lower unit price if the order is above a threshold I need to hit to make the economics work. Specifically, if you commit to 5,000 units, I can offer the price you've asked. Anything below that stays at the current rate.
pairs.negative: I can do the lower price for larger orders. The relationship is worth more than squeezing the margin on a big order.

pairs.context: A tenant is asking for a rent reduction; you want something in return.
pairs.positive: I can reduce the rent if you sign a two-year extension at the same time. A shorter-term reduction doesn't work for me, but locking in a longer term does — so I'll move on the monthly if you give me the commitment.
pairs.negative: The market is tough right now. I'll agree to the reduction and hope things improve by the next renewal.

pairs.context: A co-founder wants to reduce their hours; you need to renegotiate the equity accordingly.
pairs.positive: I'm open to you reducing your hours, but we need to adjust the equity split to reflect the change in contribution. I'm not comfortable keeping the current split if the workloads are no longer balanced. Let's agree on a formula before this change takes effect.
pairs.negative: I understand you need to reduce your hours. We can sort the equity conversation out later when things are clearer.

pairs.context: A client is requesting a penalty waiver for a project that ran late partly due to both sides.
pairs.positive: I'll waive half the penalty given that some of the delay was attributable to late approvals on your side. I'm not in a position to waive it entirely, but I think halving it is fair given the shared responsibility.
pairs.negative: I'll waive the penalty. The project is done now and I'd rather move on than get into a dispute about who caused the delay.

pairs.context: A potential partner is asking for a right of first refusal on your next fundraise.
pairs.positive: I can give you right of first refusal if it comes with a commitment to participate in the current round. I don't want to give away future optionality for nothing — it needs to be tied to real commitment now.
pairs.negative: Right of first refusal seems reasonable. It doesn't cost me anything right now and it keeps them engaged.
